title: The Work of Creation, Part 2
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: The Work of Creation_ Part 2 (Pastor Tito Lyro).txt
outline: Introduction: Reviewing creation as one way God executes His decrees
Continuing through Genesis 1 and the days of creation
The creation of light, sky, land, and seas
Vegetation, celestial bodies, and living creatures
The uniqueness of man's creation in God's image
Creation and the goodness of God
Conclusion: The significance of creation for theology
summary: Pastor Tito Lyro continues the creation series, reviewing the nature of creation and exploring the days of creation in Genesis 1. He examines what Scripture teaches about God's creative acts and the goodness of creation.
transcript:
We are going to continue our short series on creation. Remember that creation is one of the ways that God executes his decrees. In eternity past, God foreordained whatever comes to pass, and then he puts those plans into practice — in two ways: through his work of creation, and then through his work of providence.

Last week we saw that from very early on in the history of the Christian church, this was a major doctrine. The Apostles' Creed — the very first church-wide confession of faith — starts with: *"I believe in God the Father Almighty, maker of heaven and earth."* One thing the church has agreed on through the centuries is that God is the maker of all things. We also saw that the Westminster Standards — the Confession and the two Catechisms — teach that God created all things, which is a very important part of what we believe here, in our denomination, and in the broader Presbyterian and Reformed world. And then we went to the Scriptures and saw that the Bible does indeed teach that God created the world.

Let us review those passages briefly. Open your Bible to Genesis 1. The Scriptures teach that God created the universe — everything in it, not just the visible but also the invisible.

## Review: What the Scriptures Teach About Creation

Reading from Genesis 1:1–2:3, we hear the account of God's six days of creative work and his rest on the seventh. I will read it for us:

*In the beginning, God created the heavens and the earth. The earth was without form and void, and darkness was on the face of the deep, and the Spirit of God was hovering over the face of the waters...*

[The account continues through each day of creation: Day 1 — light; Day 2 — the firmament; Day 3 — dry land and vegetation; Day 4 — celestial lights; Day 5 — sea creatures and birds; Day 6 — land animals and man; Day 7 — rest.]

*Then God saw everything that He had made, and indeed it was very good.*

It is clear from the passage that God created everything. And if you keep asking why we insist on showing this from the Bible, it is because it is such an important doctrine that if we do not get it right, we are going to get a lot of other things wrong as well — including human sexuality, our understanding of who we are, what the life to come will be, and the role that God has in our lives.

Last week we also saw that God created everything *ex nihilo* — out of nothing. The Scriptures teach that too:

- **Psalm 90:2:** *"Before the mountains were brought forth, or ever You had formed the earth and the world, from everlasting to everlasting You are God."*
- **John 17:5:** Jesus prays, *"Now, Father, glorify Me in Your presence with the glory that I had with You before the world existed."*
- **Psalm 33:6:** *"By the word of the LORD the heavens were made, and by the breath of His mouth all their host."* There was nothing there before; everything was created by the word of the Lord.
- **Psalm 148:5:** *"Let them praise the name of the LORD! For He commanded and they were created."*
- **Romans 4:17:** *"[God] calls into existence the things that do not exist."*
- **Hebrews 11:3:** *"By faith we understand that the universe was created by the word of God, so that what is seen was not made out of things that are visible."*

Out of nothing, God created everything. And that last passage shows us how important this is — it is of the essence of our faith to understand and believe that God created all things out of nothing.

## The Purpose of Creation

So we have seen that God created. We have seen that he created out of nothing. Now there are other things that are even more important to consider — and one of them is the purpose of creation. Why does creation exist?

All of God's decrees are designed for his own glory, and this includes the creation of the universe — everything seen and unseen. The creation of the world does not only glorify the wisdom and power of God by its very existence, but it also provides a platform — a theater — where the glory of God can be displayed. Not only does the existence of all things glorify God, but creation provides the place where his glory is manifested in a special way, including his work of redemption.

The Scriptures say that creation reveals God's existence, his power, his wisdom, his glory, and it provides an answer to false religion and to unbelief.

**Psalm 19:1:** *"The heavens declare the glory of God, and the sky above proclaims His handiwork."* Notice the loud words: *declares* and *proclaims*. This is not a quiet whisper — creation is shouting that God exists. It takes more work to deny God's existence in the face of creation than to believe there is a God. That is why Romans 1 talks about humanity *suppressing* the truth — it is a constant suppression. The moment the unregenerate stops suppressing what creation says about God, the evidence becomes overwhelming. That is why Psalm 14 and 53 say *"the fool says in his heart, 'There is no God'"* — and it could very well be translated *keeps saying*, because the moment he stops, the evidence for God is so strong that he is convinced.

**Isaiah 40:25–26:** *"To whom then will you compare Me, that I should be like him? says the Holy One. Lift up your eyes on high and see: who created these?"*

**Romans 1:20:** *"For His invisible attributes, namely, His eternal power and divine nature, have been clearly perceived, ever since the creation of the world, in the things that have been made. So they are without excuse."*

God was pleased with his creation. Look at the pattern in Genesis 1: after each creative act, *"God saw that it was good"* — verses 4, 10, 12, 18, 21, 25 — and then at verse 31, *"God saw everything that He had made, and indeed it was very good."* Creation is good. God is pleased with creation because it displays his glory.

**Isaiah 43:7:** *"Everyone who is called by My name, whom I created for My glory, whom I formed and made."* Humanity was created for the glory of God.

**Revelation 4:11:** *"Worthy are You, our Lord and God, to receive glory and honor and power, for You created all things, and by Your will they existed and were created."*

So the primary purpose of creation is not the happiness or even the holiness of God's creatures. The primary purpose of creation is the manifestation of the glory of God himself. That is why all things exist — to praise the glory of God, to ascribe glory to God, to declare and demonstrate his glory.

## The Time of Creation: Views We Should Know

We have seen that God created, that he created out of nothing, and that he created all things for his glory. Now we come to the *time* of creation. This is an area filled with technical arguments, and it deserves more detailed study than we can provide in one lesson. But we will attempt a survey.

It is important that we understand what is offered in the Christian church for interpreting Genesis 1 and 2, and then we will spend some time on what we believe the Bible teaches.

The views can be divided into two categories. First, there are **non-historical theories** — views that say Genesis 1 and 2 do not give us any historical account. Second, there are **extended-time views** — views that say Genesis 1 and 2 are historical accounts, but the days should be interpreted as very long periods of time rather than ordinary days.

### Non-Historical Views

**The Framework Hypothesis** is very popular today, especially in Presbyterian and Reformed circles. This view teaches that Genesis simply gives in poetic form the totality, extent, and grandeur of creation, without reference to the actual order of events. It says that Genesis 1 teaches that God created — and that is all it teaches. The days have nothing to do with time; they are forms or images designed to help us understand creation.

A helpful way to understand this view: imagine you went on a trip across all fifty states, and then organized your slideshow not geographically but by category — oceans together, deserts together, mountain ranges together. That is what this view says Genesis 1 is doing: organizing creation by categories, not by chronological order.

Those who hold the framework hypothesis say there are certain inconsistencies in Genesis 1 that compel a non-literal, non-chronological interpretation. But at the end of the day, the reason for this view is that they are convinced the evidence from secular science requires them to read Genesis 1 as something other than a straightforward historical account. If the theory of evolution — both geological and biological — had never been proposed, there would be no need for the framework hypothesis. No one would think to read Genesis 1 any other way than plainly.

This is an epistemological problem: where do you get your truth? This position says your foundation for knowledge is not necessarily the Bible — you start somewhere else and then explain the Bible in terms of that. Now, sometimes science helps us understand the Bible better. But it cannot be your primary way of approaching the Scriptures.

This is, for what it is worth, the position of the majority of believing Reformed and Presbyterian people in the United States. It is the position officially taught at Covenant Seminary, at Westminster Seminary in Escondido, and at Westminster Theological Seminary in Philadelphia. It seems very scholarly and it has value for certain things, but it does betray this epistemological foundation.

**The Revelatory Day Hypothesis** is less common today but still held by some. This view teaches that the days in Genesis 1 are not the days on which God created, but the days on which God told Moses what he had created. On day one, God says to Moses, *I created light and darkness.* Moses goes home, comes back the next day. On day two, God says, *I created whatever was made on day two.* And so on. According to this view, the actual order and duration of creation is not discussed at all in the Bible.

The attraction of this position is that any view recognizing God as the cause of all things would be acceptable. But I hope you see the issues with both of these positions. The biggest problem is that when you read Genesis 1, it presents a very simple, straightforward account of creation. To say this account has no relation to the actual events would seem to deny the perspicuity of Scripture — the doctrine that the Bible is understandable. It also implies that for thousands of years, God's people had no idea what had actually happened — they were deceived by the plain reading of the text — until someone came up with evolutionary theory. That is not an acceptable way to read the Scriptures.

### Extended-Time Views

These are views that say yes, Genesis 1 is a historical account, but the days are not ordinary-length days.

**The Day-Age Theory** holds that each "day" of creation represents a very long period of time — potentially millions or billions of years. This became the view of Princeton Seminary under Charles Hodge, A. A. Hodge, and Warfield, and it was widely held in Reformed and Presbyterian circles as a result.

**The Intermittent Day Theory** holds that the days are regular 24-hour days, but there are enormous gaps of time between them — millions or billions of years between day one and day two, and so on.

**The Gap Theory** sees a break between Genesis 1:1 and 1:2. God created everything in verse 1, including a pre-Adamic human race and the dinosaurs; then destroyed that creation; and then in verse 2 he begins again with a new creation. This view was popularized by the Scofield Reference Bible in the late 1800s and early 1900s. The fossil record, in this view, comes from that original Genesis 1:1 creation.

All of these extended-time views exist for one reason: people think the evidence from secular science — particularly geological and biological evolution — is so strong that they must change the way they read Genesis 1. There is no other reason. None of these positions would have been conceived if the idea of a slowly forming universe had never been proposed.

## The View We Hold: Recent Creation

The view that we believe is most faithful to the biblical evidence is simply this: God created the universe in consecutive ordinary days, resting on the seventh day. This is the plain reading of Genesis 1 and 2, and it is what our confession teaches. When Westminster Confession chapter 4 says God created all things "in the space of six days," the authors in 1648 were not thinking this could mean billions of years.

So God created, God created out of nothing, God created for his glory, and God created in six consecutive ordinary days. How we came about impacts how we think about all of life. This is worth studying.
